message:
feat(context): Add buildContextResult function and graceful cass degradation

MAJOR ADDITIONS:

1. buildContextResult(task, rules, antiPatterns, history, warnings, queries, config)
   Assembles all context components into a structured ContextResult optimized
   for LLM consumption. Applies transformations:
   - lastHelpful: Human-readable relative time for feedback recency
   - reasoning: Extracts provenance/origin story for each bullet
   - historySnippets: Truncated and cleaned for display (max 300 chars)
   - Respects config size limits (maxBulletsInContext, maxHistoryInContext)

2. contextWithoutCass(task, config, options)
   Graceful degradation when cass binary is unavailable or searches fail.
   Provides useful context from playbook alone:
   - Loads merged playbook
   - Scores bullets by keyword relevance
   - Separates rules from anti-patterns
   - Checks deprecated patterns in task text
   - Returns empty historySnippets and suggestedCassQueries
   - Logs warning about degraded mode

3. formatLastHelpful(bullet) and extractBulletReasoning(bullet)
   Local helper implementations for transforming bullet metadata.
   Exported for testing. Uses feedbackEvents array to find most recent
   helpful event and format as relative time.

MODIFICATIONS TO contextCommand():
- Now uses generateSuggestedQueries() from utils for richer query suggestions
  (up to 5 suggestions vs previous hardcoded 2)
- Delegates result assembly to buildContextResult() for consistent
  transformations and size limit enforcement

WHY:
The context command is the primary interface for agents to retrieve relevant
rules and history. These changes improve:
- Resilience: System works even when cass is unavailable
- Richness: More context about rule provenance and recency
- Consistency: Centralized result building with proper transformations
- Discoverability: Better suggested queries for follow-up exploration

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-hzxr","title":"[Config] loadMergedConfig - Combine global + repo config","description":"Function signature:
```typescript
async function loadMergedConfig(): Promise<Config>
```

Steps:
1. Load global config (~/.cass-memory/config.json)
2. Load repo config (./.cass/config.yaml) - may not exist
3. Deep merge (repo overrides global for same keys)
4. Validate with ConfigSchema
5. Return merged config

Deep merge strategy:
- Primitive values: repo overrides global
- Objects: recursively merge keys
- Arrays: repo replaces entire array (no element merging)

Example:
Global: { llm: { provider: 'anthropic', model: 'opus' }, maxTokens: 4000 }
Repo: { llm: { model: 'sonnet' } }
Result: { llm: { provider: 'anthropic', model: 'sonnet' }, maxTokens: 4000 }

Validation ensures merged config conforms to ConfigSchema before returning.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:49:47.020793-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:49:47.020793-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-i57c","title":"[Critical] Starter Playbooks - Pre-built Rules for Common Stacks","description":"$(cat /tmp/fix_i57c.txt)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-07T17:08:18.216525-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T18:24:04.278898-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-i73","title":"[Generator] contextWithoutCass - Graceful degradation","description":"Graceful degradation when cass is unavailable - provide playbook-only context.

SIGNATURE:
async function contextWithoutCass(task: string, config: Config): Promise<ContextResult>

PURPOSE:
When cass binary is not found or cass searches fail, still provide useful context from the playbook alone. The system remains functional even without historical data.

FLOW:
1. Log warning: 'cass unavailable - providing playbook context only'
2. Extract keywords from task using extractKeywords (simple regex, no LLM)
3. Score bullets using keywords
4. Separate anti-patterns
5. Build ContextResult with:
   - relevantBullets (from playbook)
   - antiPatterns (from playbook)
   - historySnippets: [] (empty)
   - deprecatedWarnings: [] (can't check without history)
   - suggestedCassQueries: [] (no point suggesting if cass unavailable)
6. Add warning to result: 'Context generated without historical data'

KEYWORD EXTRACTION (NO LLM):
function extractKeywords(task: string): string[] {
  // Simple heuristic:
  // 1. Lowercase and split on whitespace
  // 2. Remove stop words (the, a, is, etc)
  // 3. Extract technical terms (camelCase, snake_case, etc)
  // 4. Return unique terms
}

DEGRADATION LEVELS:
1. Full: LLM queries + cass history + playbook
2. No LLM: Simple keywords + cass history + playbook
3. No cass: LLM queries + playbook only (NOT IMPLEMENTED)
4. No cass, no LLM: Simple keywords + playbook only (THIS FUNCTION)

STILL USEFUL BECAUSE:
- Playbook contains learned patterns from past work
- Scoring by keyword relevance still surfaces useful rules
- Anti-patterns still prevent common mistakes
- Better than nothing!

ERROR HANDLING:
- Don't throw errors - this IS the error handler
- If playbook also unavailable, return minimal result:
  { task, relevantBullets: [], antiPatterns: [], historySnippets: [], deprecatedWarnings: [], suggestedCassQueries: [] }

LOGGING:
- console.warn level (not error)
- Message: 'cass unavailable - showing playbook only'
- Include reason if known (binary not found vs search failed)

USE CASE:
- Fresh install before cass is set up
- CI environment without session history
- Corrupted cass index
- User working offline","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:02.102425-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T20:06:29.95556-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-07T20:05:18.929182-05:00"}
-{"id":"cass_memory_system-ia2","title":"[Cass] cassExport - Export session content","description":"Implement cassExport() to export session content in various formats.

## Signature
```typescript
async function cassExport(
  sessionPath: string, 
  format: "markdown" | "json" | "text" = "markdown",
  cassPath = "cass"
): Promise<string | null>
```

## CLI Command
`cass export "{sessionPath}" --format {format}`

## Implementation
- Use execFile() with maxBuffer: 50MB for large sessions
- Return raw output string
- Return null on error

## Error Handling
- Return null if session not found (exit 4)
- Return null if export fails
- Log error details at debug level
- No retries (export is deterministic)

## Format Options
- markdown: Claude-style markdown with headers and formatting
- json: Raw JSON session structure
- text: Plain text concatenation of messages

## Usage
Primary use case is diary generation:
```typescript
const content = await cassExport(sessionPath, "markdown");
if (content) {
  const summary = await llm.summarize(content);
  await writeDiaryEntry(summary);
}
```

## Exit Codes
- 0: Export successful
- 4: Session not found
- Other: Export error

## Performance
- Large sessions may take several seconds
- maxBuffer prevents truncation of long sessions
- Consider streaming API in future for very large sessions","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:44:58.772146-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T20:00:17.559559-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-07T20:00:14.156352-05:00"}

- Log error details at debug level
- No retries (export is deterministic)

## Format Options
- markdown: Claude-style markdown with headers and formatting
- json: Raw JSON session structure
- text: Plain text concatenation of messages

## Usage
Primary use case is diary generation:

## Performance

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-idy1","title":"[Decay] getDecayCurveData - Data for decay visualization","description":"Generate time-series data showing how a bullet's scores decay over time. Used for visualization, debugging decay parameters, and explaining score changes to users. Enables future charting/graphing features.

COMPLETE IMPLEMENTATION:
```typescript
/**
 * Generate decay curve data for visualization.
 * Shows how helpful/harmful/effective scores change over time.
 */
function getDecayCurveData(
  bullet: PlaybookBullet,
  daysToPlot: number = 180,
  stepSize: number = 7  // Weekly data points
): Array<{
  day: number;
  helpful: number;
  harmful: number;
  effective: number;
  date: string;
}> {
  const now = Date.now();
  const dataPoints: Array<{
    day: number;
    helpful: number;
    harmful: number;
    effective: number;
    date: string;
  }> = [];

  for (let day = 0; day <= daysToPlot; day += stepSize) {
    const futureTime = now + (day * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000);

    // Calculate decayed scores at this future point
    const helpful = getDecayedScore(
      bullet.helpfulEvents,
      bullet.confidenceDecayHalfLifeDays,
      futureTime
    );

    const harmful = getDecayedScore(
      bullet.harmfulEvents,
      bullet.confidenceDecayHalfLifeDays,
      futureTime
    );

    const baseScore = helpful - (4 * harmful);
    const maturityMultiplier = {
      candidate: 0.5,
      established: 1.0,
      proven: 1.5,
      deprecated: 0
    }[bullet.maturity];
    const effective = baseScore * maturityMultiplier;

    dataPoints.push({
      day,
      helpful: Math.round(helpful * 100) / 100,
      harmful: Math.round(harmful * 100) / 100,
      effective: Math.round(effective * 100) / 100,
      date: new Date(futureTime).toISOString().split('T')[0]
    });
  }

  return dataPoints;
}
```

USAGE EXAMPLES:

1. ASCII CHART (for CLI):
```typescript
const data = getDecayCurveData(bullet, 90, 7);

console.log('Effective Score Decay (next 90 days):');
console.log('');
data.forEach(point => {
  const bar = '█'.repeat(Math.max(0, Math.floor(point.effective)));
  const label = `Day ${point.day.toString().padStart(3)}`;
  console.log(`${label}: ${bar} (${point.effective.toFixed(1)})`);
});
```

Output:
```
Effective Score Decay (next 90 days):

Day   0: ████████████ (12.0)
Day   7: ███████████ (11.2)
Day  14: ██████████ (10.5)
Day  21: █████████ (9.8)
Day  28: █████████ (9.2)
Day  35: ████████ (8.6)
...
Day  84: ████ (4.1)
Day  91: ███ (3.8)
```

2. JSON FOR CHARTING:
```typescript
// Export for frontend charting library (Chart.js, D3, etc.)
const chartData = getDecayCurveData(bullet, 180, 1);

const chart = {
  labels: chartData.map(d => d.date),
  datasets: [
    {
      label: 'Effective Score',
      data: chartData.map(d => d.effective),
      borderColor: 'rgb(75, 192, 192)'
    },
    {
      label: 'Helpful (decayed)',
      data: chartData.map(d => d.helpful),
      borderColor: 'rgb(75, 192, 75)'
    },
    {
      label: 'Harmful (decayed)',
      data: chartData.map(d => d.harmful),
      borderColor: 'rgb(192, 75, 75)'
    }
  ]
};
```

3. EXPLAIN SCORE CHANGE:
```typescript
// User asks: "Why did this rule's score drop?"
const data = getDecayCurveData(bullet, 90, 30);

console.log(`Current score: ${data[0].effective}`);
console.log(`30 days from now: ${data[1].effective}`);
console.log(`60 days from now: ${data[2].effective}`);
console.log(`90 days from now: ${data[3].effective}`);
console.log(`\nScore will drop ${(data[0].effective - data[3].effective).toFixed(1)} points over 90 days due to decay.`);
```

4. COMPARE HALF-LIFE SCENARIOS:
```typescript
// Compare 60-day vs 90-day vs 120-day half-life
const halfLives = [60, 90, 120];

halfLives.forEach(halfLife => {
  const tempBullet = { ...bullet, confidenceDecayHalfLifeDays: halfLife };
  const data = getDecayCurveData(tempBullet, 180, 30);
  
  console.log(`\nHalf-life: ${halfLife} days`);
  console.log(`Score at 90 days: ${data[3].effective}`);
  console.log(`Score at 180 days: ${data[6].effective}`);
});
```

PARAMETERS:

daysToPlot (default: 180):
- How far into future to project
- 90 = quarterly view
- 180 = semi-annual view
- 365 = annual view

stepSize (default: 7):
- Granularity of data points
- 1 = daily (180 points for 180 days)
- 7 = weekly (26 points for 180 days)
- 30 = monthly (6 points for 180 days)

Trade-off:
- Smaller stepSize = more data points, smoother curve, slower computation
- Larger stepSize = fewer data points, faster, good for CLI displays

DATA POINT STRUCTURE:

day: Number of days from now (0, 7, 14, ...)
helpful: Decayed helpful score at that point
harmful: Decayed harmful score at that point
effective: Final effective score (helpful - 4×harmful) × maturity
date: ISO date string (2025-03-15)

WHY THIS IS USEFUL:

1. TRANSPARENCY:
   Users can see exactly why scores change over time

2. DEBUGGING:
   Verify decay algorithm is working correctly

3. PLANNING:
   "If I don't update this rule, it will be deprecated in 60 days"

4. EDUCATION:
   Help users understand exponential decay concept

5. TUNING:
   Compare different half-life values to find optimal settings

FUTURE ENHANCEMENTS:

1. Web Dashboard:
   - Interactive charts with Chart.js
   - Zoom, pan, hover tooltips
   - Compare multiple bullets

2. Alerts:
   - "Rule will drop below 0 in 30 days"
   - "Rule will auto-deprecate in 45 days"

3. What-If Analysis:
   - "What if I mark this helpful 3 more times?"
   - "What if I extend half-life to 120 days?"

4. Export:
   - CSV for spreadsheet analysis
   - PNG chart generation
   - PDF reports

DEPENDENCIES:
- getDecayedScore()
- PlaybookBullet with events and halfLife
- maturity multipliers

RETURNS:
Array of data points with day, helpful, harmful, effective, date

PERFORMANCE:
- O(n × m) where n = number of steps, m = number of events
- For 26 weekly points and 50 events: ~1300 calculations
- Fast enough for real-time display","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:48:11.319323-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T17:14:43.66176-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-ikds","title":"[CLI] doctor command - System health check","description":"Implements the main entry point for the 'cass-memory doctor' command - a comprehensive system health check.

COMMAND SYNTAX:
```bash
cass-memory doctor [options]

Options:
  --json    Output as JSON instead of pretty terminal UI
  --fix     Auto-fix recoverable issues (interactive confirmation)
```

PURPOSE:
Single command to verify the entire cass-memory setup is healthy and properly configured. Diagnoses common issues and offers auto-fixes where safe.

HEALTH CHECK CATEGORIES:

1. CASS INTEGRATION:
   - cass binary found in PATH
   - Version compatibility
   - Index health (stale? corrupted?)
   - Session count and storage size
   - Agent breakdown (which tools used)

2. STORAGE:
   - Data directory exists and writable
   - Disk space available (warn if <100MB)
   - Playbook file valid and loadable
   - Diary count and total size
   - Embedding cache status

3. LLM PROVIDERS:
   - OpenAI API key present and format valid
   - Anthropic API key (optional)
   - Other providers configured
   - Rate limit status (if available)

4. SELF-TEST:
   - Playbook load performance
   - Cass search latency
   - Sanitization pattern breadth
   - Config validation

IMPLEMENTATION REQUIREMENTS:
1. Parse command-line options
2. Run all health checks in parallel (where safe)
3. Collect results with pass/warn/fail status
4. Identify fixable issues
5. Render results (terminal UI or JSON)
6. If --fix: prompt and apply fixes

OUTPUT FORMAT (Terminal):
```
CASS-MEMORY SYSTEM HEALTH CHECK
================================

[✓] Cass Integration
    ✓ cass found at /usr/local/bin/cass (v1.2.0)
    ✓ Index healthy (156 sessions, last updated 2h ago)
    ⚠ Large index size (2.3GB) - consider pruning old sessions

[✓] Storage
    ✓ Data directory: /Users/me/.cass-memory
    ✓ Disk space: 45GB available
    ✓ Playbook loaded (156 bullets)
    ✗ Diary directory not found

[⚠] LLM Providers
    ✓ OpenAI API key configured
    ⚠ Anthropic key missing (optional for some features)

[✓] Self-Test
    ✓ Playbook load: 45ms
    ✓ Cass search: 120ms
    ✓ Sanitization: 23 patterns loaded

SUMMARY: 1 error, 2 warnings
  • Run with --fix to auto-repair recoverable issues
```

ERROR TYPES:
- PASS (✓): All good
- WARN (⚠): Non-critical issue
- FAIL (✗): Critical problem

FIXABLE ISSUES:
- Missing directory → create
- Missing playbook → create empty
- Stale cass index → rebuild
- Invalid config → reset to defaults

NON-FIXABLE ISSUES:
- Missing API keys → manual setup required
- Insufficient disk space → manual cleanup
- cass not found → installation required

DEPENDENCIES:
- Health check modules (checkCassIntegration, checkStorage, etc.)
- Terminal UI renderer
- Auto-fix utilities
- Config system

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Detects all common issues reliably
- Clear actionable guidance for failures
- Auto-fix safe and effective
- Performance <3 seconds for full check
- JSON output suitable for CI/CD integration","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:54:29.60623-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:54:29.60623-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-im5","title":"[Utils] generateBulletId - Unique bullet identifier generation","description":"","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:44:58.314468-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T18:48:03.203875-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-07T18:47:47.137585-05:00","comments":[{"id":18,"issue_id":"cass_memory_system-im5","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Function Signature
```typescript
function generateBulletId(): string
```

## Implementation Details
- Format: "b-{timestamp36}-{random}"
- timestamp36: Date.now().toString(36) - base36 encoding of current timestamp
- random: Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 8) - 6 chars of base36 random
- Example output: "b-lz3k5m2-x7y8z9"
- Concatenate with hyphens

## Why This Matters
Must be unique across all playbooks. Base36 keeps IDs short but readable (vs UUID which is long). Timestamp prefix ensures rough chronological ordering. Random suffix prevents collisions.

## Edge Cases to Handle
- Collision risk - very low with timestamp + 6 random chars (~2 billion combinations per ms)
- Clock skew - if system clock goes backward, IDs might not be monotonic
- Concurrent generation - random suffix handles this
- Determinism - NOT deterministic, generates new ID each call","created_at":"2025-12-07T23:15:50Z"}]}
